supporting_evidence: Clear mechanical trigger — helped friend move apartments 2 days ago
Pain reproducible on palpation of chest wall
Partial relief with ibuprofen consistent with inflammatory/musculoskeletal process
Young patient with no prior cardiac history
Timeline consistent with delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS)
Chest wall tenderness with pleuritic component
Common in young adults after physical exertion
Pain reproducible on palpation — classic finding
Partial NSAID response supports inflammatory etiology
Patient on drospirenone-containing OCP — known VTE risk factor
Pleuritic nature of chest pain is consistent with PE
SpO2 95% slightly lower than expected for age
HR 102 — tachycardia could indicate underlying cardiopulmonary process
Prior ED visit for right calf pain — possible undiagnosed DVT
Pleuritic chest pain is the hallmark symptom
Low-grade fever 37.2°C could suggest viral etiology
Young patient — viral pleurisy is common in this age group
Self-limiting condition but must exclude PE first
Young female presenting with chest pain — anxiety is statistically common
Tachycardia could reflect anxiety component
Diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out organic causes
Night-time awakening can occur with anxiety

reasoning: 28-year-old female with pleuritic chest pain after physical exertion. The reproducible chest wall tenderness and clear mechanical trigger make musculoskeletal strain the most likely diagnosis. However, the combination of OCP use and pleuritic pain mandates consideration of PE, which is flagged as a don't-miss diagnosis. SpO2 at 95% and tachycardia at 102 add mild concern. The prior ED visit for calf pain is noted as a potential precursor to DVT-related PE. D-dimer screening is recommended to exclude PE before attributing symptoms purely to musculoskeletal cause.
